enrichment.summary: Kyle gives a wide-ranging, off-the-cuff lecture on mammal evolution, covering the origins of mammals from reptiles, continental separation enabling mammalian diversity, the comet extinction 66 million years ago, and the role of humans in shaping and culling mammal populations over the past 300,000 years. He touches on specific mammal lineages (cats, dogs, elephants, horses, camels), genetic bottlenecking, island dwarfism, Neanderthals, and the origins of lactose tolerance.
enrichment.speaker: kyle

stances.position: Continental separation at the exact moment of dinosaur extinction was a lucky miracle that enabled independent mammal evolution on each landmass
Humans are primarily responsible for wiping out giant mammals, though some also perished in the Younger Dryas comet impact
Dogs have been domesticated for 300,000 years, not just the commonly cited few thousand
There are no truly wild horses anywhere — all so-called wild horses are descended from escaped domesticated stock
All cat species descend from a single genetic bottleneck survivor, explaining why they all look similar despite size range
Modern animals that tolerate humans are the survivors selected by 300,000 years of human culling of dangerous ones
Environmental awareness is not a modern concept — Romans and earlier humans already understood ecology and lamented pollution
Before about 4,000 years ago humans were more peaceful and matrilineal; overpopulation and resource competition caused the shift to patrilineal warfare
